document_text:
Task Aware Modulation using Representation Learning for Upsaling of Terrestrial
Carbon Fluxes
Aleksei Rozanov1*, Arvind Renganathan1*, Vipin Kumar1
1University of Minnesota, Twin Cities
{rozan012, renga016, kumar001}@umn.edu
Abstract
Accurately upscaling terrestrial carbon fluxes is central to
estimating the global carbon budget, yet remains challeng-
ing due to the sparse and regionally biased distribution of
ground measurements. Existing data-driven upscaling prod-
ucts often fail to generalize beyond observed domains, lead-
ing to systematic regional biases and high predictive un-
certainty. We introduce Task-Aware Modulation with Rep-
resentation Learning (TAM-RL), a framework that cou-
ples spatio-temporal representation learning with knowledge-
guided encoder-decoder architecture and loss function de-
rived from the carbon balance equation. Across 150+ flux
tower sites representing diverse biomes and climate regimes,
TAM-RL improves predictive performance relative to exist-
ing state-of-the-art datasets, reducing RMSE by 8–9.6% and
increasing explained variance (R2) from 19.4% to 43.8%, de-
pending on the target flux. These results demonstrate that in-
tegrating physically grounded constraints with adaptive rep-
resentation learning can substantially enhance the robustness
and transferability of global carbon flux estimates.
Introduction
Accurately quantifying the exchange of carbon dioxide be-
tween land and atmosphere is essential for understanding
the global carbon balance and predicting climate feedbacks
(Masson-Delmotte et al. 2021). Eddy covariance (EC) tow-
ers provide directin-situmeasurements of terrestrial carbon
fluxes such as net ecosystem exchange (NEE), gross pri-
mary production (GPP), and ecosystem respiration (RECO).
However, each tower observes only a small area (typically
a few square kilometers), making global coverage infeasi-
ble. To address this limitation, large-scale networks such as
FLUXNET (Baldocchi et al. 2001) and AmeriFlux (Novick
et al. 2018) aggregate and distribute EC data from several
hundred sites worldwide, with most located in North Amer-
ica and Europe (Fig. 1). While EC measurements provide
high-fidelity flux estimates at individual sites, their coverage
is insufficient to produce spatially continuous assessments of
global carbon sequestration needed for climate policy, car-
*These authors contributed equally.
This paper was accepted to the KGML Bridge at AAAI 2026 (non-
archival).
Copyright © 2024, Association for the Advancement of Artificial
Intelligence (www.aaai.org). All rights reserved.bon accounting, and parameterization of Earth system mod-
els.
Yet, given this data, an upscaling task can be formulated
as infering a continuous flux field from ground-level data
and gridded datasets using a non-linear estimator. Classi-
cal methods such as resampling and spatial interpolation
(bilinear, spline, kriging) perform poorly because they ig-
nore ecosystem context and temporal variability. In con-
trast, recent advances in representation learning have opened
new opportunities for integrating satellite-based observa-
tions, which provide dense spatial coverage of vegetation
and surface properties, and meteorological data to predict
carbon fluxes at regional and global scales. Combining these
complementary modalities enables data-driven models to
infer fluxes in unobserved regions. Yet, despite impres-
sive results, most ML-based upscaling frameworks remain
domain-specific – trained and evaluated on limited sets of
flux towers – and their generalization to unseen biomes and
climates remains weak.
A key reason is that flux–feature relationships are highly
context-dependent: the same satellite-derived vegetation in-
dex may indicate different physiological states under vary-
ing weather conditions. Furthermore, differences in data
resolution, noise characteristics, and temporal coverage in-
troduce covariate shifts that standard ML models cannot
handle. As a result, current approaches often excel within
known regions but fail when transferred globally.
To overcome these limitations, recent research empha-
sizes domain generalization and task-aware learning ap-
proaches that explicitly separate location-specific variability
from generalizable ecosystem dynamics. By learning invari-
ant representations that capture process-level dependencies
rather than purely statistical correlations, such methods aim
to produce models that scale robustly across ecosystems, cli-
mates, and temporal contexts.
Thus, here we propose applying the Task-Aware Mod-
ulation with Representation Learning (TAM-RL) frame-
work (Renganathan et al. 2025) to the problem of upscal-
ing of terrestrial carbon fluxes. The original TAM-RL ap-
proach combines an LSTM-based temporal encoder that
captures location-specific attributes and produces a latent
representation with a modulated decoder that generates the
final predictions. Originally developed for few-shot learn-
ing, this framework has already demonstrated strong per-arXiv:2603.09974v1  [cs.LG]  10 Mar 2026

Figure 1:Spatial distribution of the sites used in the current study
where color represents climate type according to K ¨oppen–Geiger
climate classification (Cui et al. 2021). The sites are derived from
the FLUXCOM, AmeriFlux, ICOS, and JapanFlux networks.
formance across several environmental modeling tasks. Our
work makes the following novel contributions:
• We extend TAM-RL to the domain generalization setting,
evaluating its ability to perform global carbon flux up-
scaling without site-specific fine-tuning, e.g. zero-shot;
• We design a knowledge-guided loss function incorpo-
rating the carbon balance equation (NEE=GPP−
RECO), quality-control weighting, and class balancing
across ecosystem types;
• We demonstrate significant performance gains over cur-
rent state-of-the-art analog, achieving an average RMSE
reduction of 8–9.6% and anR2increase of 19.4–43.8%
across all ecosystem types.
Overall, our results show that TAM-RL effectively addresses
the domain generalization challenge and outperforms classi-
cal tree-based models that depend on extensive manual fea-
ture engineering.
Related Works
Knowledge-Guided ML
Despite the widespread use of process-based models in
Earth system science, their performance is often limited by
structural biases, incomplete process representations, and
high computational demands. As an alternative, data-driven
machine learning (ML) approaches have gained substan-
tial traction over the past decade, offering high efficiency
during inference, flexibility in incorporating diverse input
drivers, and the ability to learn rich feature representations
– particularly when neural networks (NNs) are employed.
However, purely data-driven methods tend to overfit and ex-
hibit poor generalization in data-sparse setups. Knowledge-
Guided Machine Learning (KGML) has emerged as a
promising paradigm that combines the strengths of process-
based and data-driven modeling, enabling faster conver-
gence, improved physical consistency, and better general-
ization.
According to (Karpatne, Jia, and Kumar 2024), strate-
gies for incorporating scientific knowledge into ML can be
grouped into three main categories:
(a) Modifying the learning algorithm, for instance by em-
bedding domain constraints directly into the loss func-
tion;(b) Integrating scientific knowledge into the model architec-
ture, such as by constraining intermediate layers or de-
signing physics-informed structures;
(c) Knowledge-guided weight initialization, for example
through pre-training on outputs of physical or “surro-
gate” models.
Due to space constraints, we forgo a comprehensive re-
view of related work. KGML has been widely explored
across scientific domains, including chemistry (Ji and Deng
2021; Wu et al. 2023), physics (Cai et al. 2021; Ren et al.
2022), hydrology (Read et al. 2019; Xu and Valocchi 2015),
and climate science (Liu et al. 2024), demonstrating its
broad applicability for learning system dynamics, surrogate
modeling, and prior-informed frameworks.
Carbon Flux Upscaling
Carbon flux upscaling methods typically derive targets from
EC networks and predict GPP, RECO, and/or NEE from re-
mote sensing and meteorological inputs. The most promi-
nent framework is FLUXCOM (Jung et al. 2020) and its
successor FLUXCOM-X-BASE (Nelson et al. 2024), which
uses XGBoost (Chen and Guestrin 2016) with MODIS
and climate forcings at hourly, 0.05° resolution — we use
it as our state-of-the-art baseline. Recent work has ex-
plored meta-learning (Nathaniel, Liu, and Gentine 2023),
PFT-specialized mixture of experts (Yuan et al. 2025), and
KGML approaches (Fan et al. 2025), but to our knowledge
none has framedCO 2upscaling as a zero-shot regression
transfer learning problem.
Figure 2:TAM-RL Architecture
Methodology
Dataset
Our dataset integrates eddy covariance (EC) fluxes, MODIS
satellite observations, and ERA5-Land reanalysis data.
Ground-truth fluxes are derived from 579 EC sites (2000–
2023) across FLUXNET, AmeriFlux, ICOS (Rebmann
et al. 2018), and JapanFlux (Ueyama et al. 2025), span-
ning all K ¨oppen–Geiger climate classes (Fig. 1), follow-
ing the ONEFlux standard (Pastorello et al. 2020) for daily
GPP NTVUT USTAR50 (GPP) and NEE VUT USTAR50
(NEE) estimates with NEE VUT USTAR50 QC used as a
conitnuous quality flag ranging from 0 to 1. Vegetation prop-
erties come from MODIS, particularly MOD09GA (Ver-
mote and Wolfe 2015) and MCD12Q1 (Friedl and Sulla-
Menashe 2019), at 500 m resolution, extracted over a

Figure 3:Barplots illustrating mean RMSE of CT-LSTM, TALMSTL, TAM-RL, XGBoost and FLUXCOM-X-BASE aggregated by IGBP
and K ¨oppen climate types. The error bars are computed for classes with more than one site presenting them. NEE VUT USTAR50 QC=1.
2 km×2 km tower window via Google Earth Engine (Gore-
lick et al. 2017). Meteorological predictors are from ERA5-
Land (Mu ˜noz-Sabater et al. 2021) at 0.1° resolution. All in-
puts are harmonized to daily resolution with a 45-day se-
quence window and 15-day stride.
Loss Function
We use a composite loss enforcing data quality, class bal-
ance, and physical consistency:
L= MSE·w qc·wigbp·wkoppen +α·L flux, α= 0.1
wherew qcweights samples by their continuous NEE quality
flag,w igbpandw koppen are inverse-frequency class weights
countering spatial imbalance across ecosystem and climate
types, andL fluxpenalizes violations ofNEE = RECO−
GPP. GPP and RECO are clipped to non-negative values at
inference.
Task-Aware Modulation using Representation
Learning
We leverage the TAM-RL framework (Fig. 2). The architec-
ture consists of two main components:•Modulation network:Composed of a BiLSTM-based
task encoderEand a modulation parameter generatorG.
The encoder captures temporal information and learns
task-specific embeddingsz irepresenting the characteris-
tics of each entity (site). These embeddings are then used
by the MLP-based generatorGto produce modulation
parameters.
•Forward modelF:A standard LSTM decoder used for
conditional generation of sequential responses given the
driver data. During inference, the modulation network
provides task-specific meta-initialization forF.
Modulation is a crucial component of TAM-RL, and is
applied at two points: the input layer and the final hidden
state, using Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM):
x′=γ1Jx+β 1,
h′=γ2Jh+β 2,
wherex′andh′is input and hidden state after modulation,
xandhinput and hidden state before modulation,Jde-
notes element-wise multiplication, andγ i, βiare the learned
modulation parameters.
Training occurs in two stages:

Figure 4:Relative RMSE heatmap of XGBoost, CT-LSTM, TAMLSTM, and TAM-RL derived in comparsion to FLUXCOM-X-BASE with
NEE VUT USTAR50 QC=1.
1.Pre-training:Only the decoder network is trained with-
out using any task-specific information. By initializing
the model with these pre-trained weights, the decoder is
provided with a robust foundation that has shared knowl-
edge across the distribution for subsequent task-specific
adaptation.
2.Joint training:For each task (carbon site), a small sup-
port set is used to compute the task embedding. The mod-
ulation network converts this embedding into parameters
that adjust both the shared feature extractor and predic-
tion head. A separate query set is passed to the adapted
decoder, and gradients are backpropagated through both
networks. This enables TAM-RL to learn how to modu-
late representations for new tasks using only a few exam-
ples, without test-time fine-tuning.
During inference, historical data for each site (e.g., 2001,
2011, 2021) serves as the support set to derive site context.
The modulated base network then generates final predictions
for each task.
Results
We evaluate our models against FLUXCOM-X-BASE as
the state-of-the-art product, alongside three additional base-
lines:
•CT-LSTM: a standard LSTM augmented with one-hot
encoded static features;
•TAMLSTM: the decoder-only component of TAM-RL;
•XGBoost: trained on flattened (non-temporal) feature
vectors, configured with the same hyperparameters as
FLUXCOM-X-BASE for methodological consistency.Each neural network model is represented as a mean en-
semble over ten independently trained runs (different ran-
dom seeds), ensuring comparability with XGBoost, which is
ensemble-based by design. FLUXCOM-X-BASE provides
hourly GPP and NEE estimates, which we averaged to daily
values to match our models’ output resolution, along with
spatial resolution and grid alignment.
Models were trained on the site list used in FLUXCOM-
X-BASE and evaluated on the remaining 164 held-out
sites. Fig. 3 shows RMSE distributions across IGBP ecore-
gions and K ¨oppen–Geiger climate zones, computed on
highest-quality observations (NEE VUT USTAR50 QC=
1); error bars reflect within-category variance across sites.
Across the five dominant climate types, CT-LSTM, TAML-
STM, and TAM-RL consistently outperform XGBoost and
FLUXCOM-X-BASE, though performance degrades for
certain IGBP classes such as water bodies (WAT).
Fig. 4 presents a relative RMSE heatmap computed as
(yfluxcom−x−base −ˆy)/y fluxcom−x−base , offering a com-
parative rather than absolute view of improvement, with
site and sample counts shown per IGBP type. Fig. 5 pro-
vides per-site RMSE andR2comparisons between TAM-
RL and FLUXCOM-X-BASE: points above the diagonal in
the RMSE plot indicate improved accuracy, while points be-
low the diagonal in theR2plot indicate higher explained
variance.
Summary
Tab. 1 aggregates average performance metrics across all
sites for each target and model. TAM-RL consistently
achieves the lowest RMSE and highestR2across both tar-

Figure 5: Scatter plots of RMSE andR2of TAM-RL and FLUXCOM-X-BASE computed for every site and colored by the
Koppen climate type.
Table 1:Model performance comparison across targets. Met-
rics are averaged across all sites. NEE VUT USTAR50 QC=1 and
NEE VUT REF QC=1.
Model GPP NEE
RMSER2RMSER2
CT-LSTM 2.03 0.42 1.67 0.21
TAMLSTM 2.04 0.40 1.63 0.21
TAM-RL 1.97 0.43 1.62 0.23
XGBoost 2.17 0.34 1.76 0.16
FLUXCOM-X-BASE 2.18 0.36 1.76 0.16
gets: compared to FLUXCOM-X-BASE, our framework re-
duces RMSE by 9.6% for GPP and 8.0% for NEE, while
improvingR2by 19.4% and 43.8%, respectively.
Several limitations remain. All models, including the
XGBoost baseline, underperform on water bodies (WAT),
suggesting the current feature set lacks variables captur-
ing aquatic processes. A moderate performance drop is
also observed for mixed forests (MF), deciduous broadleaf
forests (DBF), and evergreen needleleaf forests (ENF),
where RMSE differences relative to FLUXCOM-X-BASE
range from 0.4–6.0% depending on the target.
More broadly, our analysis reveals substantial error vari-
ability across IGBP and K ¨oppen–Geiger classes. While
TAM-RL reduces this variance relative to baselines, spatial
and climatic heterogeneity remain significant challenges forreliable global carbon flux upscaling.
Conclusion
We introduced TAM-RL, a task-aware representation learn-
ing framework for zero-shot upscaling terrestrial carbon
fluxes, trained with a knowledge-guided composite loss.
Across all experiments, TAM-RL on average outperforms
FLUXCOM-X-BASE and conventional baselines, achieving
lower RMSE and higherR2for both GPP and NEE, while
effectively capturing site-specific characteristics and tempo-
ral dynamics in the presence of sparse, heterogeneous EC
data.
Challenges remain for underrepresented ecosystems, par-
ticularly water bodies and certain forest types, where perfor-
mance lags behind, and substantial error variability persists
across climate and ecoregion classes. Future work should fo-
cus on further developing methods for a better knowledge
transfer between different locations as well as investigate
variational or Bayesian extensions to better quantify and re-
duce predictive uncertainty in global carbon flux upscaling.

structure.title: Task Aware Modulation using Representation Learning for Upsaling of Terrestrial Carbon Fluxes
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the challenges of accurately upscaling terrestrial carbon fluxes due to the sparse and regionally biased distribution of ground measurements. It introduces a framework called Task-Aware Modulation with Representation Learning (TAM-RL) that combines spatio-temporal representation learning with a knowledge-guided encoder-decoder architecture to improve predictive performance. The document highlights the limitations of existing data-driven upscaling products, which often fail to generalize beyond observed domains, and demonstrates how TAM-RL can enhance the robustness and transferability of global carbon flux estimates by integrating physically grounded constraints with adaptive representation learning. The main points covered include the importance of accurately quantifying carbon dioxide exchange between land and atmosphere, the limitations of eddy covariance tower measurements, and the potential of representation learning to integrate satellite-based observations and meteorological data for predicting carbon fluxes at regional and global scales.

nodes.title: Abstract
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the challenges of accurately upscaling terrestrial carbon fluxes due to the sparse and regionally biased distribution of ground measurements. It introduces a framework called Task-Aware Modulation with Representation Learning (TAM-RL) that combines spatio-temporal representation learning with a knowledge-guided encoder-decoder architecture to improve predictive performance. The document highlights the limitations of existing data-driven upscaling products, which often fail to generalize beyond observed domains, and demonstrates how TAM-RL can enhance the robustness and transferability of global carbon flux estimates by integrating physically grounded constraints with adaptive representation learning. The main points covered include the importance of accurately quantifying carbon dioxide exchange between land and atmosphere, the limitations of eddy covariance tower measurements, and the potential of representation learning to integrate satellite-based observations and meteorological data for predicting carbon fluxes at regional and global scales.

nodes.title: Introduction
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the Task-Aware Modulation with Representation Learning (TAM-RL) framework for upscaling terrestrial carbon fluxes. The main points covered include the challenges of accurately estimating global carbon budgets due to sparse and regionally biased ground measurements, and the limitations of existing data-driven upscaling products. The document introduces TAM-RL as a solution, which combines spatio-temporal representation learning with a knowledge-guided encoder-decoder architecture and loss function derived from the carbon balance equation. The framework is evaluated across 150+ flux tower sites, demonstrating improved predictive performance and reduced errors compared to existing state-of-the-art datasets. The document also discusses the importance of domain generalization and task-aware learning approaches in overcoming the limitations of current upscaling methods, and highlights the potential of TAM-RL in enhancing the robustness and transferability of global carbon flux estimates. Additionally, the document touches on related works, including knowledge-guided machine learning and carbon flux upscaling methods, and describes the methodology and dataset used in the study.

nodes.title: Related Works
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the application of a machine learning approach, specifically TAM-RL, to the problem of carbon flux upscaling. The main points covered include the introduction of a knowledge-guided loss function, the evaluation of TAM-RL in a domain generalization setting, and the comparison of its performance to state-of-the-art models. The document also provides an overview of related works in knowledge-guided machine learning and carbon flux upscaling, highlighting the limitations of traditional process-based models and the potential of data-driven approaches. Additionally, the document describes the dataset used, which integrates eddy covariance fluxes, satellite observations, and reanalysis data from various sources, and outlines the methodology employed to evaluate the performance of TAM-RL. Overall, the document presents a novel approach to addressing the challenge of carbon flux upscaling using machine learning and highlights its potential for improving the accuracy of global carbon flux predictions.

nodes.title: Knowledge-Guided ML
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the application of a machine learning approach, specifically TAM-RL, to the domain generalization setting for global carbon flux upscaling. The main points covered include the extension of TAM-RL to the domain generalization setting, the design of a knowledge-guided loss function, and the demonstration of significant performance gains over current state-of-the-art methods. The document also provides an overview of knowledge-guided machine learning, its applications, and related works in the field of Earth system science, particularly in carbon flux upscaling. Additionally, it describes the dataset used, which integrates eddy covariance fluxes, MODIS satellite observations, and ERA5-Land reanalysis data, and outlines the methodology employed. The overall focus is on addressing the domain generalization challenge in carbon flux upscaling using a knowledge-guided approach.

nodes.title: Carbon Flux Upscaling
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the application of a machine learning approach, specifically TAM-RL, to the domain generalization setting for global carbon flux upscaling. The main points covered include the extension of TAM-RL to the domain generalization setting, the design of a knowledge-guided loss function, and the demonstration of significant performance gains over current state-of-the-art models. The document also provides an overview of knowledge-guided machine learning, its applications, and related works in the field of Earth system science, as well as the methodology and dataset used in the study. Additionally, it touches on carbon flux upscaling methods, including the FLUXCOM framework, and highlights the novelty of framing CO2 upscaling as a zero-shot regression transfer learning problem.

nodes.title: Methodology
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the application of a machine learning approach, specifically TAM-RL, to the problem of carbon flux upscaling. The main points covered include the introduction of a knowledge-guided loss function, the evaluation of TAM-RL in a domain generalization setting, and the comparison of its performance to state-of-the-art models. The document also provides an overview of related works in the field of knowledge-guided machine learning and carbon flux upscaling, highlighting the limitations of traditional process-based models and the potential of data-driven approaches. Additionally, the document describes the dataset used, which integrates eddy covariance fluxes, satellite observations, and reanalysis data from various sources, and outlines the methodology employed to evaluate the performance of TAM-RL. Overall, the document presents a novel approach to addressing the challenge of domain generalization in carbon flux upscaling and demonstrates its effectiveness in outperforming traditional models.

nodes.title: Dataset
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses the application of a novel framework, Task-Aware Modulation using Representation Learning (TAM-RL), to the domain of carbon flux upscaling. The main points covered include: 

1. The introduction of TAM-RL, which extends the existing framework to the domain generalization setting, allowing for zero-shot regression transfer learning.
2. The design of a knowledge-guided loss function that incorporates the carbon balance equation, quality-control weighting, and class balancing across ecosystem types.
3. The demonstration of significant performance gains over current state-of-the-art methods, with an average RMSE reduction of 8-9.6% and an R2 increase of 19.4-43.8% across all ecosystem types.
4. A review of related works in Knowledge-Guided Machine Learning (KGML) and its applications in various scientific domains.
5. The description of the dataset used, which integrates eddy covariance fluxes, MODIS satellite observations, and ERA5-Land reanalysis data.
6. The architecture of the TAM-RL framework, which consists of a modulation network and a forward model, and the application of Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) to the input layer and the final hidden state.

nodes.title: Loss Function
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses the evaluation and implementation of various machine learning models, including CT-LSTM, TALMSTL, TAM-RL, XGBoost, and FLUXCOM-X-BASE, for predicting ecosystem characteristics. The main points covered include: 
1. The use of bar plots to compare the mean RMSE of different models aggregated by IGBP and Köppen climate types.
2. The utilization of a composite loss function that enforces data quality, class balance, and physical consistency.
3. The implementation of the Task-Aware Modulation using Representation Learning (TAM-RL) framework, which consists of a modulation network and a forward model.
4. The application of Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) to the input layer and the final hidden state.
5. A two-stage training process for the TAM-RL model, incorporating meteorological predictors from ERA5-Land and inputs harmonized to daily resolution.

nodes.title: Task-Aware Modulation using Representation Learning
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the development and evaluation of a task-aware modulation using representation learning (TAM-RL) framework for predicting net ecosystem exchange (NEE) and gross primary productivity (GPP) across different sites and climate types. The document presents the architecture of the TAM-RL framework, which consists of a modulation network and a forward model, and explains the training process in two stages: pre-training and joint training. The results show that TAM-RL outperforms other models, including CT-LSTM, TAMLSTM, XGBoost, and FLUXCOM-X-BASE, in terms of root mean squared error (RMSE) and R-squared (R2) across different International Geosphere-Biosphere Programme (IGBP) ecosystem types and Köppen-Geiger climate zones. The document also presents visualizations of the results, including bar plots, heatmaps, and scatter plots, to compare the performance of the different models. Overall, the document highlights the effectiveness of the TAM-RL framework in improving the accuracy of NEE and GPP predictions.

nodes.title: Results
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of several machine learning models, including XGBoost, CT-LSTM, TAMLSTM, and TAM-RL, for predicting carbon site data, specifically NEE (Net Ecosystem Exchange) values. The models are compared to the state-of-the-art product FLUXCOM-X-BASE, with TAM-RL showing consistent improvement in terms of RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) and R2 (coefficient of determination) across different climate types and IGBP (International Geosphere-Biosphere Programme) ecoregions. The document also describes the training process for TAM-RL, which involves pre-training and joint training with a small support set to adapt to new tasks. The results are presented in various figures and tables, highlighting the performance of each model and the advantages of TAM-RL.

nodes.title: Summary
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a task-aware representation learning framework called TAM-RL for zero-shot upscaling of terrestrial carbon fluxes. The main points covered include the introduction of TAM-RL, its pre-training and joint training processes, and its evaluation against state-of-the-art models such as FLUXCOM-X-BASE, CT-LSTM, TAMLSTM, and XGBoost. The results show that TAM-RL outperforms the baseline models in terms of RMSE and R2 for both GPP and NEE targets, with an average reduction in RMSE of 9.6% for GPP and 8.0% for NEE, and an improvement in R2 of 19.4% and 43.8%, respectively. However, the document also highlights the limitations of the framework, including underperformance on water bodies and certain forest types, and substantial error variability across climate and ecoregion classes. Future work is suggested to focus on developing methods for better knowledge transfer and investigating variational or Bayesian extensions to reduce predictive uncertainty in global carbon flux upscaling.

nodes.title: Conclusion
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the performance of a task-aware representation learning framework, TAM-RL, in upscaled terrestrial carbon fluxes. The main points covered include a comparison of TAM-RL's performance with other models, such as FLUXCOM-X-BASE and XGBoost, across different targets, including Gross Primary Productivity (GPP) and Net Ecosystem Exchange (NEE). The results show that TAM-RL outperforms the baselines, reducing Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) by 9.6% for GPP and 8.0% for NEE, while improving R2 by 19.4% and 43.8%, respectively. However, the document also highlights limitations, including underperformance on water bodies and certain forest types, and substantial error variability across climate and ecoregion classes. The conclusion emphasizes the need for further development to address these challenges and improve knowledge transfer between different locations.

nodes.title: References
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the introduction of a task-aware representation learning framework, TAM-RL, for zero-shot upscaling of terrestrial carbon fluxes. The framework is trained with a knowledge-guided composite loss and outperforms conventional baselines, including FLUXCOM-X-BASE, in terms of reducing RMSE and improving R2 for both GPP and NEE. The document presents results from experiments, including scatter plots and model performance comparisons, and highlights the challenges of underrepresented ecosystems, such as water bodies and certain forest types. The conclusion emphasizes the need for further development of methods to improve knowledge transfer between locations and to quantify and reduce predictive uncertainty in global carbon flux upscaling.
